hypothesis: We believe performance advertisers lose 15-20% campaign efficiency to manual pacing adjustments because DSPs don't expose real-time budget reallocation controls designed for non-specialist users, resulting in systematic overspend in low-performing windows and underspend in high-converting windows.

assumptions.statement: Manual pacing adjustments are a regular, time-consuming part of the campaign management workflow
Budget waste from poor pacing is visible and measurable to practitioners
Existing DSP pacing controls are too crude or too complex for mid-market teams to use effectively
Teams have enough data to make intelligent real-time budget reallocation decisions
Users can interpret real-time pacing alerts and act on them without specialist support
Platform APIs expose real-time spend and performance data at the granularity needed for automated pacing
Automated budget changes via API won't trigger ad account review flags or platform restrictions
The efficiency gain from better pacing is large enough to justify a separate tool subscription

scripts.raw_text:
1. Describe how you currently manage budget pacing across your campaigns.
2. How much time per week do you spend making manual pacing adjustments?
3. Don't you think manual pacing is one of the biggest sources of wasted ad spend?
4. What happens when a campaign underpaces â€” do you have a standard response process?
5. Have you ever calculated how much budget waste comes from poor pacing? What did you find?
6. Would you trust an automated system to adjust your campaign budgets in real-time without your approval?
7. What would have to happen for you to feel confident in an automated pacing tool?
8. How do the pacing controls in your current DSP fall short of what you actually need?
9. If you had perfect pacing, what other problems would still be holding back your campaign performance?
10. What's the worst pacing disaster you've had to clean up? What caused it?

sessions.raw_notes:
P1: Senior Campaign Manager, travel brand, ~180 employees. Experienced, very hands-on.

- Manages 20-30 live campaigns simultaneously across DV360, Meta, and The Trade Desk.
- Pacing check frequency: 'I check pacing at 9am, 1pm, and 4pm every day. It's part of my routine.'
- Pain: 'On Friday afternoons when budgets need to clear by end of day, it gets really stressful. You're basically refreshing dashboards and adjusting bids every 30 minutes.'
- Waste estimate: 'If I had to guess, maybe 10-15% of budget goes to the wrong place because of pacing. We front-load when we should back-load and vice versa.'
- Current workaround: 'I use Google Sheets with API pulls to track cumulative spend vs. ideal burn curve. It's taken me 2 years to build and it's fragile.'
- Automation trust: 'I'd want human oversight. Not fully automated. A system that recommends changes and I approve them â€” that I'd use.'
- Platform gap: 'DV360 has flight-level pacing but it's all or nothing. Either the platform controls pacing or I do. There's no middle ground.'

P2: Performance Marketing Manager, fintech startup, ~60 employees. Sceptical about the pain.

Pushed back on the core hypothesis early: 'Our campaigns are relatively small â€” Â£50k/month total across Meta and Google. At this scale, pacing is genuinely not a big deal. I check it maybe once a day, make a small adjustment every couple of weeks. It's not a significant time sink.'

Identified a different primary pain: 'For us the bigger issues are audience saturation and creative fatigue. I burn through an audience in about 3 weeks and then performance drops off a cliff. That's where I spend my mental energy, not on pacing.'

Did acknowledge one seasonal exception: 'Black Friday is a completely different story. That week I'm checking pacing every 2 hours, adjusting bids manually, panicking. So the pain is real â€” it's just concentrated into a few days per year, not a weekly problem.'

Relied heavily on platform automation: 'Meta's own campaign budget optimisation is actually pretty good at this spend level. I set a daily budget, let it run, and it distributes sensibly. For Â£50k/month I'm not sure a pacing tool would add much on top of what Meta already does.'

Offered useful scale threshold insight: 'I do think if we scaled to Â£200k/month across 4-5 platforms, pacing would become a real operational problem. Right now it's not in my top 10. Come back to me in 18 months.'

Would not pay: 'My discretionary tool budget is tiny. I'd rather spend it on creative production than on infrastructure for a problem I don't really have yet.'

P3: Programmatic Trading Specialist, agency, manages multiple mid-market clients.

- Manages Â£800k/month across 15 clients on DV360 and TTD.
- 'Pacing is genuinely my biggest operational headache. I have to context-switch between clients constantly.'
- Key insight: 'The worst thing is when a campaign underpaces for two weeks and then the client asks why we didn't spend their budget. There's no good answer.'
- Current tools: 'Sizmek had a pacing tool. DSPs have basic controls. Nothing stitches it together across platforms.'
- Automation view: 'I'd automate straightforward pacing adjustments â€” change flight end date, extend budget on overperforming campaigns. But anything involving bid changes needs human approval.'
- 'The real problem is cross-platform pacing. If DV360 underpaces, I want to shift that budget to TTD automatically. That's not possible today.'
- Would pay: 'Absolutely. If this saved my team 10 hours a week I'd pay Â£2,000/month.'
- Feasibility concern: 'I've heard platform APIs have rate limits that make real-time adjustments difficult. Have you tested this?'

P4: VP Digital, consumer goods brand, ~400 employees. Strategic perspective, less hands-on.

- Team does the day-to-day pacing. Her view is high-level.
- 'My team tells me pacing is a pain. But honestly it's not a reason we lose clients or miss targets â€” it's just operational overhead.'
- Interesting perspective: 'The real cost isn't wasted budget. It's the opportunity cost of my team's attention. They should be spending time on strategy, not refreshing dashboards.'
- Has tried automation before: 'We piloted an automated bidding tool two years ago. It worked but we had to turn it off after it spent Â£20k in 3 hours when a campaign settings changed unexpectedly.'
- Trust barrier: 'Any automated budget tool needs hard budget caps and kill switches. Non-negotiable. The agency panic story is the horror story every CMO has heard.'
- Would buy: 'Yes, but procurement needs audit trails and explainability. Show me why the system made each decision.'

P5: Independent programmatic consultant. Challenges both the problem framing and the solution.

- 'Pacing problems are real but they're often symptoms of bad campaign setup, not bad tools.'
- 'If a campaign consistently underpaces, the problem is usually the targeting is too narrow, the bid is too low, or the creative is getting rejected. Fixing pacing automation doesn't fix those.'
- 'Platform algorithms are actually getting better at pacing. Meta's campaign budget optimisation is legitimately good. DV360 improved their algorithm last year.'
- Agrees on cross-platform gap: 'The place where automation genuinely helps is across platforms. No single platform can optimise for you across all of them. That's a real gap.'
- 'I'd be cautious about building autonomous budget changes into this. Every programmatic trader I know has a horror story about automated systems spending uncapped in a few minutes.'
- 'The product I'd want is anomaly detection and alerts, not automated changes. Tell me when something is going wrong. Let me decide what to do about it.'

P6 — VP Programmatic Trading, holding company agency, 1,200 employees. Manages pacing for a portfolio of 40+ clients, total media under management $120M.

Has a dedicated trading desk with 12 programmatic traders. Each trader manages 8-12 clients simultaneously. Pacing is checked three times daily via a custom dashboard built on DV360 API + Google Sheets.

"At our scale, pacing is a discipline, not an afterthought. We have SLAs with clients that require within-5% budget delivery. Underdelivery means we refund media. Overdelivery means we absorb the overage."

In a typical week, approximately 30% of campaigns trigger at least one manual adjustment.

"That's 30% of campaigns requiring human intervention, across 40 clients, three times a day. That's a lot of touches."

Has evaluated automated pacing tools including Quantcast, MediaMath's pacing algorithms, and a custom solution their tech team built. The custom solution was the most effective but required a full-time engineer to maintain.

"We built it, it worked, the engineer left, and we couldn't support it anymore. Went back to manual."

Would pay for a managed pacing service that provides guaranteed delivery within SLA, with penalties on the vendor side for misses.

"I don't want software. I want a service that owns the outcome. If you're confident your tool works, take the delivery risk."

Confirming on pain at scale. Challenging on software-only solutions — wants outcome-based pricing.
sessions.summary: Agency VP managing $120M in media across 40 clients where 30% of campaigns require manual pacing intervention three times daily. Tried a custom engineering solution that worked but wasn't maintainable. Key insight: at agency scale, the buyer wants outcome-based pricing with vendor-side delivery guarantees — not software licensing — because client SLA risk sits with the agency, not the tool.

sessions.raw_notes:
P7 — Senior Campaign Manager, DTC brand, 180 employees. Recently churned from automated pacing within DV360 — switched back to manual pacing 4 months ago.

Used DV360's built-in automated pacing for 8 months. Turned it off after it caused two significant budget misallocation incidents.

"The first incident: DV360's algorithm shifted 60% of a campaign budget into display in the last 3 days of the month because display was hitting CPA targets. But our brand guidelines prohibit heavy display in the run-up to a product launch. The algorithm didn't know that context."

"The second incident: automated pacing accelerated spend when a competitor's PR crisis broke. Our ads appeared next to the crisis news coverage. The algorithm saw cheap CPMs and bought aggressively. Brand safety nightmare."

Has gone back to manual pacing with daily check-ins.

"Manual is slower but it's controllable. I understand what it's doing and I can override it. The automated system did things I didn't understand and couldn't predict."

Would consider automation again but with stronger constraint controls.

"I want to automate within guardrails I set. No more than X% budget shift per day, brand safety exclusion lists must be respected, specific placement types capped. If the tool can't honor those constraints, I don't want it."

His current manual process: Excel spreadsheet tracking planned vs. actual spend daily, with campaign-level delivery report pulled from DV360 each morning. Total time: 45-60 minutes per day.

Challenging on fully autonomous pacing. Would accept automation with strict constraint parameters.
sessions.summary: Campaign Manager who churned from DV360's automated pacing after two costly incidents — a budget misallocation that violated brand guidelines, and an aggressive spend acceleration into brand-unsafe content. Has returned to manual pacing for control. Would re-adopt automation only with strict guardrails: per-day budget shift limits, brand safety constraint honoring, placement type caps. Key insight: autonomous pacing without constraint parameters creates unacceptable brand risk.

sessions.raw_notes:
P8 — Head of Media Finance, media company, 350 employees. Responsible for reconciling planned vs. actual campaign spend across all media vendors.

Spends approximately 3 days per month on pacing reconciliation work — comparing trafficking orders to vendor invoices to actual delivery reports. Finds discrepancies in 40-60% of campaigns that need manual investigation.

"Most discrepancies are small — within 3-5%. But I've had $80,000 overbilling incidents that took two weeks to resolve because the vendor's delivery report didn't match our tracking system."

The biggest reconciliation pain: multi-vendor campaign buys where a single campaign is split across three DSPs.

"Each DSP has a different flight date, a different reporting currency, and a different way of counting impressions. Reconciling a three-way split campaign is a 6-hour project every time."

Would not describe herself as close to the pacing execution problem — that's the media team's problem. Her problem is downstream financial accuracy.

"I don't care how the pacing is managed, as long as I get clean delivery data that matches the invoice. That almost never happens."

Her ask: an automated reconciliation tool that ingests delivery reports from multiple DSPs, normalizes them to a common standard, and flags discrepancies before invoices arrive.

"If I could catch a $50K discrepancy before it becomes an invoice dispute, that would save weeks of work."

No interest in pacing automation per se. Strong interest in financial reconciliation automation. Would pay $500-1,000/month.

Uncovers an adjacent market: financial reconciliation tooling for media buys is a distinct pain from pacing optimization.
sessions.summary: Media Finance Head spending 3 days/month reconciling planned vs. actual spend with discrepancies in 40-60% of campaigns, including an $80K overbilling incident. Her problem is downstream from pacing — financial reconciliation accuracy. Uncovers an adjacent market: financial reconciliation tooling for media buys at $500-1K/month, distinct from pacing optimization itself.

sessions.raw_notes:
P9 — Performance Marketing Manager, e-commerce company, based in Dubai, operating across GCC and South Asia. Manages campaigns across multiple currencies (AED, SAR, PKR, INR).

Currency volatility compounds pacing complexity significantly. When a campaign is budgeted in AED but media is purchased in USD, a 3% currency move mid-flight changes the effective budget available without any campaign action.

"I've had campaigns overspend by 8% because the AED weakened against the dollar mid-month and I didn't catch it for three days."

Platform fragmentation in the region: Meta and Google are primary, but Snapchat is disproportionately important in Saudi Arabia, and regional platforms like Anghami have growing relevance. Each platform reports spend in different currencies.

"I'm looking at six dashboards in three currencies and trying to figure out if I'm on pace. There's no unified view."

Ramadan is the biggest pacing challenge: CPMs spike 3-5x in the two weeks before Ramadan, then drop immediately after.

"Every year I underestimate the Ramadan CPM spike. I set budgets based on historical data but the spike is getting more extreme each year. I always burn budget too fast in the run-up."

His manual solution: a Google Sheets model that converts all spending to USD daily, tracks against flight plan, and sends him a WhatsApp alert if any campaign is more than 10% off pace.

"It works but it's fragile. Any time a platform changes its reporting format my sheet breaks."

Confirming on pacing pain in multi-currency, multi-platform environment. Challenging on most pacing tools being US-centric and not handling currency conversion.
sessions.summary: GCC-based marketer where pacing complexity is compounded by multi-currency exposure (AED budgeted, USD purchased) and seasonal Ramadan CPM spikes of 3-5x. Uses a fragile Google Sheets model with WhatsApp alerts as his pacing monitor. Most commercial pacing tools are US-centric and don't handle currency conversion or regional seasonal dynamics. Key insight: the international pacing problem has unique compounding factors that Western tools rarely address.

sessions.raw_notes:
P10 — Programmatic Operations Manager, performance agency, 60 employees. Has built a custom pacing dashboard in Looker Studio connected to DV360, Meta, and Google Ads APIs.

Spent 6 weeks building the dashboard after becoming frustrated that each platform's native reporting had different refresh rates and different pacing calculations. The custom dashboard normalizes spend data hourly, calculates expected vs. actual delivery, and sends Slack alerts when any campaign is more than 8% off pace.

"The dashboard tells me everything I need to know. The problem is what it tells me is always that something needs my attention. I'm still responding manually to every alert."

Current limitation: the dashboard identifies pacing issues but the fix always requires a human to log into the platform and make the adjustment.

"I get the alert at 7pm. I log into DV360. I move the budget. I check again at 9pm. It's reactive, not proactive."

Has explored using the DV360 API to automate the budget adjustment itself. Got 80% of the way there but stopped because of the risk of runaway automated adjustments.

"I don't trust automation to make budget decisions without a human review step. What if there's a data quality issue in the API and my automation accidentally moves $50,000?"

His ideal system: automated detection + recommendation, with a one-click approval flow in Slack.

"If Slack tells me 'Campaign X needs $2,000 moved from placement A to placement B — approve?' and I can say yes in one tap, that's 80% of the manual work removed with human oversight preserved."

Strongly confirming on pain. Has built the detection layer himself. Needs the human-in-loop automated execution layer.
sessions.summary: Programmatic Ops Manager who built a custom pacing dashboard detecting issues across DV360, Meta, and Google Ads — but fixes are always manual because he doesn't trust fully autonomous budget adjustments. His ideal workflow: automated detection + recommendation + one-click Slack approval for execution. This is the most specific product spec in the study: human-in-the-loop pacing automation, not fully autonomous adjustment.
